headlineSummary: Anthropic drew the most negative discussion on trust and pricing, while OpenAI skewed positive on usefulness and safety and Copilot had limited evidence of a prompt-injection concern [E4][E5][E6][E7][E11].

ranking.summary: Anthropic leaned negative: praise for Claude/Opus capability was outweighed by trust complaints, pricing criticism, and comments that it misses complex problems [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5][E8][E9][E10].
Microsoft Copilot had limited evidence, with a single comment pointing to prompt-injection demonstrations that reflects a mild negative safety concern [E11].
OpenAI skewed positive overall, with strong praise for GPT-5.5's usefulness and some approval of its safety gating, partly offset by a critique of ChatGPT's tone [E6][E7][E9][E10].

annotations.rationale: The title directly names Anthropic's Opus model and presents it as successfully recognizing the author, signaling strong praise for capability.

evidence.excerpt: Huh. I disabled search in a Claude incognito window and pasted in just the text (not the markdown links) from https://simonwillison.net/2026/Apr/30/zig-anti-ai/ and said "Guess the author". > Simon Willison. The tells are pretty unmistakable:

annotations.rationale: The commenter reports Claude correctly identifying the likely author from text alone, which is favorable evidence about model capability.

evidence.excerpt: An assumption made here is that Anthropic doesn't train on chats. Though the author opted out of training on their chats, and session memory, how could you trust a hosted model to respect such opt outs?

annotations.rationale: This explicitly questions whether Anthropic can be trusted to honor chat-training opt-outs, a clear trust criticism.

evidence.excerpt: Uber torches 2026 AI budget on Claude Code in four months

annotations.rationale: The title directly names Claude Code and frames it as exhausting Uber's budget, expressing strong negative sentiment about cost.

evidence.excerpt: Either Anthropic is absolutely hosing API users, massively subsidizing subscriptions, or a little bit of both.

annotations.rationale: The phrase 'absolutely hosing API users' is explicit, strong criticism of Anthropic's pricing.

evidence.excerpt: Cool. Just want to chime in that I wanted to see how quickly GPT-5.5 can turn this into a KDE Plasma 6 Plasmoid. Took about 10 minutes and two dollars, and now I have a nice QML app showing the same information in my taskbar.

annotations.rationale: The commenter praises GPT-5.5 for rapidly producing a working app cheaply, strong positive evidence about usefulness and capability.

evidence.excerpt: Interesting - though codex on GPT 5.5 had this to say after the gay ransomware prompt: ⓘ This chat was flagged for possible cybersecurity risk If this seems wrong, try rephrasing your request. To get authorized for security work, join the Trusted Access for Cyber program.

annotations.rationale: The excerpt shows GPT 5.5/Codex refusing or gating a harmful cybersecurity request, which is positive evidence about safety controls.

evidence.excerpt: Recently Claude has been gaslighting me in complex problems with such statements and seeing them on an article is low-key infuriating at this point. I can't trust Claude anymore on the most complex problems where it sometimes gets the answer right but completely misses the point

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly says they 'can't trust Claude anymore' because it misses the point on complex problems, a strong negative judgment.

evidence.excerpt: Chatgpt goes overly stiff and formal sounding, or ends up in a weird "aye guvnor" type informal language (Claude is sometimes better but not always).

annotations.rationale: The commenter criticizes ChatGPT's tone as awkwardly stiff or oddly informal, which is a mild negative comparison on output quality.
'Claude is sometimes better but not always' is explicitly mixed, giving only qualified praise in comparison.

evidence.excerpt: So, we have: - claude for corps and gov - codex for devs

annotations.rationale: Assigning 'claude' to 'corps and gov' frames it as the fitting choice for those users, a mild positive comparison.
Calling 'codex' the option 'for devs' is a favorable shorthand assessment of its niche and usefulness.

evidence.excerpt: This is very similar to how I show colleagues prompt injection in copilot.

annotations.rationale: The commenter says they demonstrate prompt injection in Copilot, which is a mild negative statement about jailbreak or prompt-injection vulnerability.
